narration_segments.text: The body remembers before the mind does. On the first morning after a great loss, the eyes open to a world that looks identical to yesterday. The ceiling is the same ceiling. The light through the curtains falls at the same angle. The alarm sounds at the same pitch. Everything is precisely as it was. And everything has changed. The body registers this change as heaviness. A gravitational pull that begins in the center of the chest and radiates outward — into the shoulders, which curl forward as though shielding the heart.

Ananya sits on the edge of her bed at dawn. The room is still dark. She has not slept — not truly — in eleven days. Eleven days since the phone call. Eleven days since the word "gone" entered her vocabulary as a permanent resident rather than a visitor. Her husband of nineteen years. A Tuesday morning. A commute he had made four thousand times. A intersection he had crossed without incident for a decade. And then the call. Ananya's body responded before her mind could organize a thought. Her legs gave way. Her vision narrowed to a tunnel. A sound left her throat — not a word, not a cry, something more ancient than language, a sound that belongs to every woman who has ever received that call in every century of human existence. She crumpled against the kitchen counter. The phone fell. His voice was replaced by the voice of a stranger saying words that had no meaning: impact, hospital, time of death, next of kin. Eleven days later, the ceremonies complete, the relatives returned to their cities, the refrigerator still full of food that neighbors brought, Ananya sits on the edge of the bed and feels nothing.

Begin with the breath. Sit in a position that feels sustainable — a chair, a cushion, the edge of a bed. The spine does not need to be perfectly erect. It needs to be supported. Place the hands on the knees, palms facing upward, in the gesture of receiving. Or place them on the heart, one on top of the other, in the gesture of devotion. Let the body choose. Close the eyes. Turn the attention inward. Feel the breath as it enters the nostrils — the slight coolness of the inhale, the slight warmth of the exhale. Follow the breath down into the chest. Notice where it stops. In a grieving body, the breath often stops high — at the collarbones, at the upper ribs. It does not descend into the belly.

Ananya is washing rice when the opening comes. Four months have passed. She has practiced daily — the breath, the mantra, the green light. Some days the practice lasts twenty minutes. Some days it lasts three. Some days she sits on the cushion and the tears come before the mantra does, and the practice becomes simply crying, which is its own form of pranayama, its own discipline of breath and release and surrender. The rice runs clear. She pours it into the pot.
